cataloging_subject.label: The English Works of Wyclif Hitherto Unprinted
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The edition-level electronic and editorial compilation represented by University of Michigan CME item AEH6713.0001.001: Frederic David Matthew's 1880 Early English Text Society volume of twenty-eight numbered Middle English tracts historically attributed to John Wyclif. The record keeps the twenty-eight medieval tract children, their disputed and multiple authorships, Matthew's editorial matter, the physical manuscripts, the 1880 printed edition, later reprint carrier, U-M encoding, linked but untranscribed page images, and repository XML distinct. It does not describe Wyclif's complete English oeuvre or imply that any one witness contains the whole compilation.

catalog_summary.title: The English Works of Wyclif Hitherto Unprinted
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous and disputed Wycliffite writers; traditionally attributed to John Wyclif
catalog_summary.date: Late fourteenth to early fifteenth century; Matthew edition published 1880

titles.value: The English Works of Wyclif Hitherto Unprinted
The English Works of Wyclif Hitherto Unprinted. Edited by F. D. Matthew
English Works of Wyclif Hitherto Unprinted

agents.name: Anonymous radical Wycliffite scholar of the early fifteenth century

notes: Matthew selected, ordered, introduced, and annotated the twenty-eight numbered tracts and supplied the back matter.

scope.description: The twenty-eight numbered medieval tracts under the edition's umbrella attribution

notes: The title and U-M discovery field preserve the historical attribution. Current Parker cataloging treats the principal manuscript collection as multi-author, and this record does not transfer the umbrella attribution into twenty-eight secure authorships.

scope.description: Collective description of the multi-author Wycliffite tract collection; individual assignments remain unresolved

notes: This collective responsibility neither claims that every tract is anonymous nor rules out Wyclif's authorship of an individual tract.

Hudson identifies Matthew pp. 362–404 with an earlier sermon by the anonymous scholar; the more securely dated Tractatus de Oblacione Iugis Sacrificii is a later work by that author, not the same text.
This is the publication date of the edition-level compilation, not a medieval composition date.

date_statements.display: Matthew dates tract I to about 1383 from its reference to Bishop Despenser's crusade
notes: The normalized year records Matthew's historical argument and awaits modern tract-specific review.

date_statements.display: Tract XXVI predates March 1413 and is probably early fifteenth century
notes: Hudson says the same anonymous scholar had already treated clerical temporalities in this sermon before composing a different tract between March 1413 and February 1414; the evidence does not date tract XXVI itself to 1413–1414.
The statement does not transfer the production place of a particular surviving manuscript to every tract.
London is the title-page place of publication, not the composition place of the medieval tracts.

scope.description: Modern English appears in title matter, Matthew's arguments, summaries, notes, and reference matter
Latin appears in tract titles, epigraphs, authorities, formulas, and quoted passages

form_statements.description: Prose compilation. Tract I's editorial argument quotes one two-line verse passage; the quotation does not make the medieval tract or compilation mixed-form.

scope.description: Prayer expositions and instruction on faith, hope, and charity
Tracts on clerical, pastoral, ecclesial, and papal office

subject_statements.label: Church reform, clerical authority, and religious orders

scope.description: Tracts addressing clerical possessions, benefices, dominion, secular office, and tithes
Prayer expositions and tracts on scripture, confession, virtues, and the Eucharist

subject_statements.label: Authorship, attribution, and the construction of the Wycliffite corpus

summaries.value: Matthew's 1880 Early English Text Society volume gathers twenty-eight numbered Middle English tracts concerned with religious orders, clerical authority and poverty, scripture, prayer, confession, sacraments, dominion, pastoral office, and the papacy. The title preserves a historical Wyclif attribution, but the principal CCCC MS 296 collection is now cataloged as multi-author and individual tract attributions remain uneven. Tract XXVI can be associated with the anonymous radical Wycliffite scholar studied by Anne Hudson, without importing the 1413–1414 date of that scholar's later work.

The XML verbally encodes the title and printer matter plus the twenty-eight numbered tracts on printed pp. 1–482, including Matthew's tract arguments and inline notes. Other preliminary matter and the back appendix, notes, and glossarial index on pp. 483–572 survive in the digital object as linked PB/EPB page-image milestones but have no OCR-transcribed words in the XML. The three empty page-wrapper paragraphs are not counted as content.

extent.description: Twenty-eight numbered prose tracts on printed pp. 1–482. Within the tracts the XML contains 28 DIV1, 262 DIV2, 4 DIV3, 322 HEAD, 499 P, 483 PB, 436 MILESTONE, 1,381 NOTE1, and 27 TABLE elements. The only L elements are two lines inside one Q1 quotation in tract I's editorial argument. Across the file there are 31 DIV1: two empty front-matter page wrappers, twenty-eight tracts, and one empty back-matter wrapper; 638 PB and 639 EPB milestones link the larger scanned book.

content_parts.label: Encoded title and printer matter
content_parts.summary: The 1880 title page and printer statement are verbally encoded.

content_parts.label: Page-linked preliminary matter not transcribed in the XML
content_parts.summary: The frontispiece and the remaining preliminary leaves through p. li are represented by linked page-image milestones but contain no OCR-transcribed verbal text in the XML.

locators.value: frontispiece and preliminary matter through p. li, excluding the separately encoded title page and printer verso

notes: Presence as linked PB/EPB milestones is not treated as verbal transcription.

content_parts.label: Twenty-eight numbered medieval tracts
content_parts.summary: The edition's twenty-eight numbered Middle English religious, devotional, ecclesiological, and polemical tracts, each preceded by Matthew's editorial argument where supplied.

content_parts.label: I. Of the Leaven of Pharisees
content_parts.summary: A polemic against hypocrisy and the religious orders, organized in eleven chapters; its editorial argument includes a two-line Chaucer quotation.

content_parts.label: II. How Men Ought to Obey Prelates
content_parts.summary: On the limits of obedience to prelates and the priority of divine law.

content_parts.label: III. The Rule and Testament of St. Francis
content_parts.summary: A vernacular treatment of the Franciscan Rule and Testament directed against later departures from Franciscan poverty.

content_parts.label: IV. Of Prelates
content_parts.summary: An extended polemic on clerical office, wealth, authority, and reform.

content_parts.label: V. Speculum de Antichristo
content_parts.summary: A short mirror of Antichrist focused on corrupt clerical and religious practice.

content_parts.label: VI. Of Clerks Possessioners
content_parts.summary: A forty-chapter argument against endowed and property-holding clergy.

content_parts.label: VII. How the Office of Curates Is Ordained of God
content_parts.summary: A thirty-three-chapter account of pastoral office, preaching, and clerical responsibility.

content_parts.label: VIII. The Order of Priesthood
content_parts.summary: A twenty-nine-chapter treatment of priestly office and discipline.

content_parts.label: IX. Three Things Destroy This World
content_parts.summary: A short tract identifying destructive abuses in ecclesiastical and social life.

content_parts.label: X. Of Feigned Contemplative Life
content_parts.summary: A polemic against counterfeit contemplative religion; Matthew restores a passage displaced within CCCC MS 296.

content_parts.label: XI. The Paternoster
content_parts.summary: A vernacular exposition of the Lord's Prayer.

content_parts.label: XII. The Ave Maria
content_parts.summary: A vernacular exposition of the Ave Maria.

content_parts.label: XIII. How Satan and His Children Turn Works of Mercy Upside Down and Deceive Men Therein and in Their Five Wits
content_parts.summary: A three-chapter treatment of works of mercy and the five senses.
notes: Matthew's contents assigns the opening to p. 210, while the tract division and page milestones begin at p. 209.

content_parts.label: XIV. How Religious Men Should Keep Certain Articles
content_parts.summary: Instruction and polemic concerning the obligations of members of religious orders.

content_parts.label: XV. Of Servants and Lords
content_parts.summary: A social and religious treatment of reciprocal duties between servants and lords.

content_parts.label: XVI. Why Poor Priests Have No Benefice
content_parts.summary: A three-chapter defense of poor priests and critique of the benefice system.

content_parts.label: XVII. How Antichrist and His Clerks Travail to Destroy Holy Writ
content_parts.summary: A five-chapter defense of scripture against ecclesiastical obstruction.
notes: Matthew's contents prints p. 253, while the tract division and page milestones begin at p. 254.

content_parts.label: XVIII. How Satan and His Priests and His Feigned Religious Cast by Three Cursed Heresies to Destroy All Good Living and Maintain All Manner of Sin
content_parts.summary: A four-chapter polemic against three alleged heresies used to sustain clerical abuse.

content_parts.label: XIX. Of Poor Preaching Priests
content_parts.summary: A short defense of itinerant poor preachers.

content_parts.label: XX. Augustinus (translation fragment)
content_parts.summary: A brief Middle English translation fragment attributed in the edition heading to Augustine.

content_parts.label: XXI. Of Dominion
content_parts.summary: A four-chapter tract on lordship and dominion.

content_parts.label: XXII. Tractatus de Pseudo-Freris
content_parts.summary: An eight-chapter polemic against false friars.

content_parts.label: XXIII. Of Confession
content_parts.summary: A tract on confession, clerical mediation, and penitential practice.

content_parts.label: XXIV. Of Faith, Hope and Charity
content_parts.summary: A six-chapter exposition of the three theological virtues.

content_parts.label: XXV. De Sacramento Altaris
content_parts.summary: A short tract on the sacrament of the altar.

content_parts.label: XXVI. The Clergy May Not Hold Property
content_parts.summary: An anonymous radical Wycliffite tract against clerical property, followed on pp. 393–404 by Matthew's internally titled appendix on secular office and authorities.
notes: The appendix on pp. 393–404 is internal to numbered tract XXVI and is not counted as a twenty-ninth medieval work.

content_parts.label: XXVII. De Officio Pastorali
content_parts.summary: A thirty-two-chapter treatise on pastoral office, preaching, tithes, and clerical poverty.

content_parts.label: XXVIII. De Papa
content_parts.summary: A twelve-chapter polemic on the pope and papal office.

content_parts.label: Editorial appendix on papal citations
content_parts.summary: Extracts from two Latin tracts concerning references to popes, printed in Matthew's appendix but not verbally transcribed in the XML.
notes: The pages are linked as images; their words are absent from the XML text stream.

content_parts.label: Editorial notes
content_parts.summary: Matthew's back-of-volume notes, linked by page images but not verbally transcribed in the XML.

content_parts.label: Glossarial index
content_parts.summary: The edition's glossarial index, linked by page images but not verbally transcribed in the XML.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source XML for aeh6713
evidence.summary: Establishes repository identity, U-M identifier, compact bibliographic metadata, OCR and encoding statement, and exact encoded content.

evidence.citation: Structural and textual inspection of aeh6713 XML against Matthew's edition
evidence.locator: TITLEPAGE; FRONT and BACK PB/EPB milestones; BODY/DIV1 1–28; element and line counts
evidence.summary: Verifies the 28-work boundary, printed-page mapping, chapter structure, two quoted verse lines, empty front/back wrappers, and the distinction between verbally encoded matter and pages represented only by image milestones.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, The English Works of Wyclif Hitherto Unprinted
evidence.summary: Confirms stable item identity, inherited Wyclif author field, 1880 source-edition citation, 2006 uncorrected OCR and Level-1 TEI production, page-image linkage, and the current public-domain / No Copyright–United States assertion.

evidence.citation: Frederic David Matthew, ed., The English Works of Wyclif Hitherto Unprinted, EETS Original Series 74 (London: Trübner, 1880)
evidence.locator: title page; contents; editorial arguments; printed pp. 1–572
evidence.summary: Direct source for the compilation boundary, titles, page ranges, editorial summaries, historical attributions, and Matthew's tract-level statements about base and collated manuscripts.

evidence.citation: Parker Library on the Web, Corpus Christi College Cambridge MS 296
evidence.summary: Confirms the current MS 296 identity, late-fourteenth-century post-1383 date, multi-author Wycliffite characterization, tract contents, and complete exposed IIIF object.

evidence.citation: University of Manchester Library, John Rylands English MS 86
evidence.summary: Confirms the current shelfmark, former Ashburnham designation, date, physical description, and exact folio loci of tracts XXIII, XXVII, and XXVIII.

evidence.citation: Anne Hudson, “A Wycliffite Scholar of the Early Fifteenth Century,” Studies in Church History Subsidia 4 (1985)
evidence.locator: discussion and note identifying Matthew pp. 362–404
evidence.summary: Attributes the sermon represented by most of tract XXVI to an anonymous radical Wycliffite scholar and establishes that it preceded the scholar's separately dated 1413–1414 tract.
Records edition, encoding, manuscript, facsimile, catalog, access, rights, and tract-scoped source relations.

open_questions.question: What are the current specialist attribution and composition-date assessments for each of the twenty-eight tracts?
next_steps: Review tract-level bibliographies and modern editions, preserving Matthew's opinions as attribution history rather than present consensus.

open_questions.question: Why does Matthew's front manuscript list associate Sidney Sussex A.4.12 with no. XX when the tract XII argument explicitly says that the Ave Maria was collated with that manuscript and tract XX says it comes only from CCCC MS 296?
next_steps: Inspect MS 74 and modern handlists to test whether the front-list 'No. XX' is a typographical error; retain only the explicit tract-XII collation relation meanwhile.

open_questions.question: What production decision caused the U-M XML to link but not OCR-transcribe most front matter and all back matter on printed pp. 483–572?
next_steps: Request item-level production notes from U-M while preserving the directly observed encoded-versus-image-linked boundary.

notes: The title and inherited U-M author field are historical attribution evidence, not proof that Wyclif authored all twenty-eight tracts.
The record distinguishes medieval composition, manuscript production, Matthew's 1880 edition, a provider-described later reprint carrier, U-M's 2006 OCR/TEI encoding, and the repository artifact.
Element totals are representation metrics from the XML, not critical-text extents.
A witness used only for collation is bound as a related source and is not silently promoted to base witness.
